nativeRange.habitat: Found along the banks of small forest streams, growing attached to rocks or rooted in mud at the water's edge, often partially emergent.

decorPreferences: porous rock (lava, dragon stone) for attachment
open vertical space for tall arrowhead leaves

summary: Generally considered the tallest Anubias in common trade, with distinctive spear-tip arrowhead leaves on long stalks. Generally regarded as an easy, slow-growing epiphyte best suited to background placement attached to driftwood or rock.
careNotes: Anubias hastifolia is generally considered the largest Anubias species in the trade, commonly reaching 30–50 cm or more in established aquariums — significantly taller than the more compact Anubias barteri or the miniature Anubias nana. The long-stalked, spear-tip arrowhead leaves make it a strong choice for the background of a tank. Like all Anubias, it is an epiphyte (a plant that grows anchored to surfaces rather than rooted in soil): the rhizome — the thick horizontal stem from which the leaves sprout — should be tied or glued to driftwood or rock using fishing line or cyanoacrylate gel (super glue), or wedged into a crevice, and must be kept ABOVE the substrate. Burying the rhizome typically causes it to rot. The species tolerates a wide range of light levels, but growth is slow regardless, and it is commonly reported to accumulate algae under high light; low-to-medium light is generally the safer default. CO2 supplementation is optional but tends to improve growth rate and reduce algae pressure. Water temperature around 22–26 °C with stable, well-cycled conditions is generally advised — sudden parameter swings have been reported to trigger leaf melt. Because the plant draws nutrients from the water column rather than the substrate, liquid fertilization with iron and trace elements is helpful. In the wild, Anubias hastifolia grows along forested stream banks in West and Central Africa, from Ghana and Nigeria through Cameroon and Gabon to the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Angola.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Tropica's plant-detail pages returned an XSLT/MacroEngine server error during direct WebFetch on the access date; data attributed to Tropica was confirmed via Tropica-referenced summaries surfaced through web search. Cross-verification performed against Aquarium Co-Op, Buce Plant, and Fish Laboratory.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Confirmed slow growth, low-light tolerance, epiphyte (super-glue-to-hardscape) habit, background placement, and tall-Anubias size class.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (15-40 cm / 6-16 in), pH 6.0-7.5, easy care level, rhizome propagation, and the warning that unstable parameters cause melt/rot.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Araceae), temperature 22-28°C, pH 6.0-7.5, slow growth (~1 leaf/month), and propagation via rhizome division.

additional.name: Kew Plants of the World Online
additional.notes: Authoritative native-range reference: West Tropical Africa to Angola (Ghana, Nigeria, Cameroon, Gabon, DRC, Angola).
